docs.title: DocsForge
docs.text:
Write your documentation in Markdown. Build a professional static site in seconds. Deploy anywhere.

pip install docsforge
docsforge serve

docs.title: What makes DocsForge different
docs.text:
• Zero Dependencies

    Everything is bundled. pip install docsforge gets you the engine, Material theme, all plugins, all extensions, fonts, icons, and math rendering.

  • Zero Config

    Start with just site_name:. All 7 plugins and 31 Markdown extensions load automatically. Customize only when you need to.

  • Math Works

    Write $$...$$ and it renders. KaTeX is vendored — no CDN, no extra_javascript, no setup.

  • Syntax Highlighted

    Code blocks render with Pygments colors at build time. No client-side JavaScript needed.

  • Instant Search

    Full-text search built in, powered by a client-side Lunr.js index.

  • Dark Mode

    Light/dark toggle in the header. Auto-detects system preference.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
pip install docsforge
docsforge new my-docs
cd my-docs
docsforge serve

That's it. Your documentation site is now running at localhost:8000.

docs.title: What you get
docs.text: Feature Status Admonitions (!!! note) ✅ Zero config Math ($$...$$) ✅ Zero config Code highlighting ✅ Zero config Tables ✅ Zero config Task lists (- [x]) ✅ Zero config Footnotes ([^1]) ✅ Zero config Definition lists ✅ Zero config Abbreviations ✅ Zero config Content tabs ✅ Zero config Diagrams (Mermaid) ✅ Zero config Emojis ✅ Zero config Blog ✅ Zero config Tags ✅ Zero config Search ✅ Zero config Privacy (self-host assets) ✅ Zero config Minify (HTML/CSS/JS) ✅ Zero config

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Getting Started

    Installation, first steps, and basic configuration

  • Setup Guides

    Customize colors, fonts, navigation, search, and more

  • Reference

    Markdown syntax, components, and formatting options

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
my-docs/
├── docsforge.yml      # Site configuration
├── docs/                # Your documentation
│   ├── index.md
│   └── ...
└── site/                # Build output (auto-generated)

docs.title: The docs/ directory
docs.text:
Every .md file becomes a page. Subdirectories become sections.

docs/
├── index.md
├── getting-started.md
├── guides/
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── install.md
│   └── configure.md
└── reference/
    ├── index.md
    └── api.md

docs.title: The docsforge.yml file
docs.text:
Minimal config:

site_name: My Project

With navigation:

site_name: My Project

nav:
  - Home: index.md
  - Guides:
    - Installation: guides/install.md
    - Configuration: guides/configure.md
  - Reference: reference/index.md

docs.title: Writing content
docs.text: DocsForge supports all Material-flavored Markdown out of the box. No extension config needed.

docs.title: Admonitions
docs.text:
!!! note "Note"
    This is a callout.

??? warning "Click to expand"
    This is collapsible.

docs.title: Math
docs.text:
Inline: $E = mc^2$

Display:
$$\sum_{i=1}^n x_i$$

docs.title: Code blocks
docs.text:
```python
def hello():
    print("Hello")

### Tables, task lists, footnotes, definition lists

All work without configuration. See [Reference](reference/index.md) for full syntax.

## Building

```bash
docsforge serve      # Dev server with live reload
docsforge build      # Production build to site/

docs.title: Publishing
docs.text:
DocsForge builds static HTML. Deploy site/ anywhere:

  • GitHub Pages: See publishing guide
  • Netlify, Vercel: Drag and drop site/
  • Your own server: rsync -av site/ server:/var/www/docs

docs.title: Features
docs.text: DocsForge includes everything you need to write and publish documentation. No extra installs, no extra config.

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text:
• Zero Dependencies

    pip install docsforge gets you the engine, Material theme, all plugins, all Markdown extensions, KaTeX math, Pygments highlighting, fonts, and icons. Nothing else to install.

  • Zero Configuration

    Start with a working site in seconds. 7 plugins and 31 Markdown extensions load automatically. Customize only what you need.

  • Light & Dark Mode

    Automatic system preference detection with a manual toggle. Both modes are fully customizable with your brand colors.

  • Full-Text Search

    Client-side search powered by Lunr.js. No external services, no backend required. Works offline.

  • Math Rendering

    Write $$...$$ and it renders with KaTeX. No CDN, no extra_javascript, no configuration.

  • Syntax Highlighting

    Code blocks render with Pygments colors at build time. Supports all major languages.

docs.title: Markdown Extensions
docs.text:
All enabled by default. No configuration needed.

Category Extensions Structure toc, tables, fenced_code, def_list, footnotes, md_in_html, meta Text admonition, abbr, attr_list, nl2br, sane_lists, wikilinks pymdownx arithmatex, betterem, caret, critic, details, emoji, escapeall, extra, fancylists, highlight, inlinehilite, keys, magiclink, mark, pathconverter, progressbar, quotes, saneheaders, smartsymbols, snippets, striphtml, superfences, tabbed, tasklist, tilde

docs.title: Plugins
docs.text:
All enabled by default. No plugins: config needed.

Plugin What it does blog Blogging with authors, categories, archives info Admonition callouts (note, tip, warning, danger) meta OpenGraph metadata minify Compress HTML/CSS/JS output privacy Self-host external assets (Google Fonts, CDN scripts) search Full-text search with Lunr.js tags Tag system with tag pages

docs.title: Publishing
docs.text:
DocsForge builds static HTML. Deploy site/ anywhere:

  • GitHub Pages — Ready-to-use workflow
  • Netlify, Vercel — Drag and drop
  • Your own server — rsync site/ server:/var/www/docs

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text: DocsForge is a self-contained documentation engine. pip install, write Markdown, build.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install docsforge

Requires Python 3.10+.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
# Create a new project
docsforge new my-docs
cd my-docs

# Start the dev server
docsforge serve

# Build for production
docsforge build

docs.title: What's inside
docs.text:
After docsforge new, your project looks like this:

my-docs/
├── docsforge.yml    # Site configuration
├── docs/
│   └── index.md     # Homepage
└── site/            # Build output

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The smallest valid docsforge.yml:

site_name: My Project

That's it. All plugins, extensions, and theme settings use sensible defaults. Add configuration only when you need to customize.

docs.title: Key defaults
docs.text: Feature Default Theme Material (built-in) Plugins search, tags, blog, info, meta, minify, privacy Markdown extensions 31 total — all pymdownx + python-markdown Math KaTeX (vendored, $$...$$ works) Code highlighting Pygments (colored syntax) Dark mode Light/dark toggle in header Fonts Self-hosted (privacy plugin downloads Google Fonts)

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Creating your site
  • Reference

docs.title: Vendored Components
docs.text:
DocsForge bundles the following open source components, each under their respective licenses:

Component License MkDocs BSD-2-Clause Material for MkDocs MIT Python Markdown BSD-3-Clause Pymdown Extensions MIT Pygments BSD-2-Clause Lunr.js MIT Mermaid.js MIT Material Design Icons Apache-2.0 Twemoji CC-BY-4.0

All vendored components are permissively licensed and compatible with commercial use.

docs.title: Documentation Content
docs.text:
The documentation text and examples in this site are licensed under CC BY 4.0.

You are free to: - Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format - Adapt — remix, transform, and build upon the material for any purpose, even commercially

Under the following terms: - Attribution — You must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text: DocsForge exists because we believe documentation should be as easy as possible.

docs.title: The problem
docs.text:
Modern docs tools require too much setup. What should be "write Markdown, get a website" often becomes:

  • Configure 10+ Markdown extensions
  • Install a theme separately
  • Add plugins one by one
  • Set up CDN scripts for math
  • Configure client-side JS for syntax highlighting
  • Debug why something broke after an update

docs.title: 1. Zero config for core features
docs.text:
Everything you need works without configuration.

Write $$...$$ → math renders. Write !!! note → admonition appears. Write ```python → code highlights. All 31 Markdown extensions and 7 plugins are loaded by default.

You configure only what you want to customize, not what you need to exist.

docs.title: 2. Self-contained
docs.text:
After pip install docsforge, you own everything.

The theme, all plugins, all extensions, KaTeX, fonts, and Pygments are bundled inside the package. No CDN calls at runtime. No external fetches during build. Works offline.

docs.title: 3. Stable
docs.text:
Your docs build the same way in 10 years.

Because everything is vendored in the package, version-pinning the package pins the entire toolchain. No transitive dependency surprises.

docs.title: 4. Material quality
docs.text:
DocsForge is Material for MkDocs, made simpler.

We didn't reinvent the theme. We took the world's most popular documentation theme and made it work without configuration. You get the same professional look, the same responsive layout, the same dark mode — just without the setup.

docs.title: What we removed
docs.text: Feature Why removed typeset Users can write Unicode directly optimize Requires external pngquant binary social Requires Pillow + CairoSVG projects Niche multi-project feature offline Privacy plugin covers most use cases group Plugin orchestrator (niche)

docs.title: What we changed
docs.text: Before (Material/MkDocs) After (DocsForge) Config file docsforge.yml docsforge.yml Theme via mkdocs.themes docsforge.themes Manually list all extensions 31 loaded by default Manually list all plugins 7 loaded by default extra_javascript for KaTeX KaTeX vendored, zero config Client-side JS for highlighting Pygments at build time

docs.title: Our goal
docs.text: pip install docsforge, write Markdown, done.

docs.title: Publishing your site
docs.text: DocsForge builds a completely static site — no database, no server-side processing, no runtime dependencies. This means you can host it anywhere that serves static files.

docs.title: GitHub Pages (recommended)
docs.text: The fastest way to get your docs online. DocsForge includes a ready-to-use GitHub Actions workflow.

docs.title: 1. Push to GitHub
docs.text:
Create a repository and push your docs project:

git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial documentation"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/my-docs.git
git push -u origin main

docs.title: 2. Enable GitHub Pages
docs.text:
1. Go to your repository on GitHub
  2. Click Settings → Pages
  3. Under Build and deployment, select Source: GitHub Actions

docs.title: 3. The workflow is already included
docs.text:
Your DocsForge starter includes .github/workflows/pages.yml. It runs automatically on every push to main:

.github/workflows/pages.yml
name: Deploy Docs to GitHub Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: pages
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.x'

      - name: Build site
        run: docsforge build

      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: site/

  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

That's it. Your site will be live at https://username.github.io/my-docs/ within a minute.

docs.title: Custom domain
docs.text:
Add a CNAME file to your docs/ directory:

docs/CNAME
docs.example.com

Then configure your DNS provider with a CNAME record pointing to username.github.io.

docs.title: Drag and drop
docs.text:
1. Run docsforge build
  2. Drag the site/ folder to Netlify Drop

docs.title: Git integration
docs.text:
1. Push your repo to GitHub
  2. In Netlify, click Add new site → Import an existing project
  3. Choose your GitHub repository
  4. Set build command: docsforge build
  5. Set publish directory: site/

docs.title: Vercel
docs.text:
1. Push your repo to GitHub
  2. In Vercel, click Add New... → Project
  3. Import your repository
  4. Override the build settings:
  • Build Command: docsforge build
  • Output Directory: site/

docs.title: Cloudflare Pages
docs.text:
1. Push your repo to GitHub
  2. In Cloudflare Pages, click Create a project
  3. Connect your GitHub account and select your repository
  4. Set build command: docsforge build
  5. Set build output directory: site/

docs.title: Amazon S3 + CloudFront
docs.text:
For enterprise deployments:

Build and sync to S3
docsforge build
aws s3 sync site/ s3://my-docs-bucket --delete
aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id ABCD --paths "/*"

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
If you prefer containerized builds:

Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /docs
COPY . .
RUN docsforge build
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=0 /docs/site /usr/share/nginx/html

docs.title: Offline distribution
docs.text:
Because DocsForge sites are fully static, you can also distribute them as a ZIP file:

docsforge build
zip -r my-docs.zip site/

Recipients can open site/index.html directly in their browser — no server required.

docs.title: Build configuration for different hosts
docs.text: Some hosts require specific settings. Adjust docsforge.yml accordingly:

docs.title: GitHub Pages (project site)
docs.text: site_url: https://username.github.io/repository-name/

docs.title: GitHub Pages (user/organization site)
docs.text: site_url: https://username.github.io/

docs.title: Subdirectory deployment
docs.text:
site_url: https://example.com/docs/

The site_url is important for: - Generating correct absolute URLs - Enabling the site_url metadata for plugins - Ensuring search and navigation work correctly

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Setup Guides — Customize your site before publishing
  • Building an optimized site — Enable minification and compression

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
All notable changes to DocsForge are documented in this file.

The format is based on Keep a Changelog, and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Zero-config Markdown — 31 extensions loaded by default (all pymdownx + python-markdown). No markdown_extensions: config needed.
  • KaTeX math — Vendored KaTeX (1.5MB) renders $$...$$ inline and display math. No CDN, no config.
  • Pygments highlighting — Syntax-colored code blocks at build time. No client-side JS.
  • Dark mode toggle — Light/dark mode switch in header. Auto-detects system preference.
  • Auto-loaded plugins — search, tags, blog, info, meta, minify, privacy all work without config.
  • Self-hosted fonts — Privacy plugin downloads and caches Google Fonts locally.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• Config file renamed from properdocs.yml to docsforge.yml
  • Theme namespace changed from mkdocs.themes to docsforge.themes
  • Plugin system — 6 plugins removed, 7 remain as built-in defaults

docs.title: Removed
docs.text:
• typeset — Users can write Unicode directly
  • optimize — Requires external pngquant binary
  • social — Requires Pillow + CairoSVG
  • projects — Niche multi-project feature
  • offline — Privacy plugin covers most use cases
  • group — Plugin orchestrator (niche)

docs.title: Added
docs.text: • Initial release

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: Complete syntax reference for all DocsForge Markdown extensions and components.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
DocsForge extends standard Markdown with powerful syntax for technical documentation. All extensions are enabled by default in the starter template.

  • Admonitions

    Note, warning, tip, danger, and custom callout boxes.

  • Annotations

    Add comments and explanations to code blocks.

  • Code blocks

    Syntax highlighting, line numbers, titles, copy button, and more.

  • Content tabs

    Group related content with tabbed interfaces.

  • Data tables

    Sortable, styled tables with alignment and formatting.

  • Diagrams

    Mermaid.js flowcharts, sequence diagrams, and more.

  • Formatting

    Highlight, subscript, superscript, and inline formatting.

  • Icons & Emojis

    8,000+ icons and 3,000+ emojis via :material-heart: syntax.

  • Images

    Captions, alignment, lazy loading, and figure markup.

  • Lists

    Ordered, unordered, nested, task lists, and definition lists.

  • Tooltips

    Abbreviations, definitions, and hover tooltips.

docs.title: Markdown extensions enabled by default
docs.text:
markdown_extensions:
  - abbr
  - admonition
  - attr_list
  - def_list
  - footnotes
  - md_in_html
  - toc:
      permalink: true
  - pymdownx.arithmatex
  - pymdownx.betterem
  - pymdownx.caret
  - pymdownx.details
  - pymdownx.emoji
  - pymdownx.highlight
  - pymdownx.inlinehilite
  - pymdownx.keys
  - pymdownx.magiclink
  - pymdownx.mark
  - pymdownx.smartsymbols
  - pymdownx.superfences
  - pymdownx.tabbed
  - pymdownx.tasklist
  - pymdownx.tilde

docs.title: Standard Markdown
docs.text:
DocsForge supports all standard Markdown syntax:

  • Headings (# to ######)
  • Paragraphs and line breaks
  • Bold (**text**) and italic (*text*)
  • Strikethrough (~~text~~)
  • Links ([text](url)) and images (![alt](url))
  • Blockquotes (> quote)
  • Inline code (`code`) and code blocks
  • Horizontal rules (---)
  • Ordered and unordered lists

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Pick a reference page above for detailed syntax and examples.

docs.title: Admonitions
docs.text: Admonitions (also called callouts or aside boxes) highlight important information with colored boxes and icons.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
!!! type "Optional title"
    Content goes here. Must be indented.

docs.title: Note
docs.text:
!!! note
    This is a note admonition.

Note

This is a note admonition.

docs.title: Tip
docs.text:
!!! tip
    This is a tip admonition.

Tip

This is a tip admonition.

docs.title: Warning
docs.text:
!!! warning
    This is a warning admonition.

Warning

This is a warning admonition.

docs.title: Danger
docs.text:
!!! danger
    This is a danger admonition.

Danger

This is a danger admonition.

docs.title: Info
docs.text:
!!! info
    This is an info admonition.

Info

This is an info admonition.

docs.title: Success
docs.text:
!!! success
    This is a success admonition.

Success

This is a success admonition.

docs.title: Abstract
docs.text:
!!! abstract
    This is an abstract admonition.

Abstract

This is an abstract admonition.

docs.title: Collapsible admonitions
docs.text:
Add ? to make the admonition collapsible:

??? note "Click to expand"
    This content is hidden by default.
Click to expand

This content is hidden by default.

Add + to make it expanded by default:

???+ note "Already expanded"
    This content is visible by default.
Already expanded

This content is visible by default.

docs.title: Custom titles
docs.text:
!!! note "Custom title here"
    You can replace the default type label.

Custom title here

You can replace the default type label.

docs.title: Without icon
docs.text:
!!! note ""
    No icon, just the title and content.

No icon, just the title and content.

docs.title: Nested content
docs.text:
Admonitions can contain any Markdown, including code blocks:

!!! example "Configuration example"
    ``` yaml
    theme:
      palette:
        primary: teal
    ```

Configuration example

theme:
  palette:
    primary: teal

docs.title: All admonition types
docs.text: Type Color Icon Use for note Blue Info circle General information abstract Cyan Document Summaries, abstracts info Blue Info Detailed information tip Green Lightbulb Helpful advice success Green Check Positive outcomes question Orange Help circle Questions, FAQs warning Orange Alert triangle Caution needed failure Red X circle Something failed danger Red Flash Critical warnings bug Red Bug Bug reports example Purple List Examples quote Grey Quote Quotations

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Code blocks
  • Content tabs

docs.title: Annotations
docs.text: Annotations add explanations, comments, and callouts directly to code blocks.

docs.title: Line numbers
docs.text:
Enable line numbers in code blocks:

``` python linenums="1"
def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")  # (1)!
    return True  # (2)!
```

1.  Greet the user with a personalized message
2.  Return success status

def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")  # (1)!
    return True  # (2)!
  1. Greet the user with a personalized message
  2. Return success status

docs.title: Highlighting lines
docs.text:
Highlight specific lines:

``` python hl_lines="2 4"
def process(data):
    result = []      # Normal
    for item in data:  # Highlighted
        value = item * 2  # Normal
        result.append(value)  # Highlighted
    return result
```

def process(data):
    result = []      # Normal
    for item in data:  # Highlighted
        value = item * 2  # Normal
        result.append(value)  # Highlighted
    return result

docs.title: Inline annotations
docs.text:
Add markers in code that reference footnotes:

``` yaml
theme:
  features:
    - navigation.tabs  # (1)!
    - search.highlight  # (2)!
```

1.  Enable tabbed navigation
2.  Highlight search terms in results

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.tabs  # (1)!
    - search.highlight  # (2)!
  1. Enable tabbed navigation
  2. Highlight search terms in results

docs.title: Copy to clipboard
docs.text:
Code blocks get a copy button automatically when content.code.copy is enabled:

theme:
  features:
    - content.code.copy

docs.title: Code block title
docs.text:
Add a title bar to code blocks:

``` yaml title="docsforge.yml"
site_name: My Project
```
docsforge.yml
site_name: My Project

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Code blocks
  • Content tabs

docs.title: Code blocks
docs.text: DocsForge supports rich code blocks with syntax highlighting, titles, line numbers, and more.

docs.title: Basic code blocks
docs.text:
Use triple backticks with a language identifier:

``` python
def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")
```

def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

docs.title: Supported languages
docs.text:
DocsForge supports 300+ languages via Pygments, including:

  • python, py
  • javascript, js
  • typescript, ts
  • bash, sh, shell
  • yaml, yml
  • json
  • html
  • css
  • markdown, md
  • dockerfile
  • sql
  • rust
  • go
  • java
  • c, cpp
  • csharp, cs
  • ruby, rb
  • php
  • lua
  • r
  • julia
  • kotlin
  • swift
  • dart

docs.title: Code block with title
docs.text:
``` python title="hello.py"
def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")
```
hello.py
def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

docs.title: Line numbers
docs.text:
``` python linenums="1"
def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")
    return True
```

def hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")
    return True

docs.title: Highlighting lines
docs.text:
``` python hl_lines="2 3"
def hello(name):
    message = f"Hello, {name}!"
    print(message)
    return message
```

def hello(name):
    message = f"Hello, {name}!"
    print(message)
    return message

docs.title: Diff blocks
docs.text:
Show additions and deletions:

``` diff
  def hello(name):
-     print("Hello!")
+     print(f"Hello, {name}!")
      return True
```

  def hello(name):
-     print("Hello!")
+     print(f"Hello, {name}!")
      return True

docs.title: Console blocks
docs.text:
Show command output:

``` console
$ docsforge build
INFO    -  Cleaning site directory
INFO    -  Building documentation to directory: site
INFO    -  Documentation built in 2.34 seconds
```

$ docsforge build
INFO    -  Cleaning site directory
INFO    -  Building documentation to directory: site
INFO    -  Documentation built in 2.34 seconds

docs.title: No copy button
docs.text:
Disable the copy button for a specific block:

``` python
```

Or globally disable in config:

theme:
  features:
    # Don't include - content.code.copy

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Annotations
  • Content tabs

docs.title: Content tabs
docs.text: Content tabs group related content, letting readers switch between alternatives without scrolling.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
Use === to define tabs:

=== "Tab 1"
    Content for tab 1. Must be indented.

=== "Tab 2"
    Content for tab 2. Must be indented.

docs.title: Example: Installation methods
docs.text:
=== "pip"
    ``` bash
    pip install mypackage
    ```

=== "conda"
    ``` bash
    conda install mypackage
    ```

=== "Docker"
    ``` bash
    docker pull mypackage:latest
    ```
pip 
pip install mypackage
conda 
conda install mypackage
Docker 
docker pull mypackage:latest

docs.title: Example: Operating systems
docs.text:
=== "Linux"
    ``` bash
    docsforge serve
    ```

=== "macOS"
    ``` bash
    docsforge serve
    ```

=== "Windows"
    ``` powershell
    docsforge serve
    ```
Linux 
docsforge serve
macOS 
docsforge serve
Windows 
docsforge serve

docs.title: Example: Programming languages
docs.text:
=== "Python"
    ``` python
    def greet(name):
        return f"Hello, {name}!"
    ```

=== "JavaScript"
    ``` javascript
    function greet(name) {
        return `Hello, ${name}!`;
    }
    ```

=== "Rust"
    ``` rust
    fn greet(name: &str) -> String {
        format!("Hello, {}!", name)
    }
    ```
Python 
def greet(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"
JavaScript 
function greet(name) {
    return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}
Rust 
fn greet(name: &str) -> String {
    format!("Hello, {}!", name)
}

docs.title: Nested tabs
docs.text:
Tabs can contain any Markdown, including other tabs:

=== "Setup"
    === "Linux"
        ``` bash
        sudo apt install python3
        ```

    === "Windows"
        ``` powershell
        winget install Python.Python.3.11
        ```

=== "Configuration"
    Edit `docsforge.yml` to customize your site.

docs.title: Tab labels with icons
docs.text:
Use icons in tab labels:

=== ":material-linux: Linux"
    Linux-specific content.

=== ":material-apple: macOS"
    macOS-specific content.

=== ":material-microsoft-windows: Windows"
    Windows-specific content.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Content tabs require the pymdownx.tabbed extension:

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.tabbed:
      alternate_style: true

The alternate_style option provides the modern tabbed interface.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Code blocks
  • Admonitions

docs.title: Data tables
docs.text: DocsForge renders Markdown tables with enhanced styling.

docs.title: Basic table
docs.text:
| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `GET` | Retrieve a resource |
| `POST` | Create a new resource |
| `PUT` | Update a resource |
| `DELETE` | Remove a resource |
Method Description GET Retrieve a resource POST Create a new resource PUT Update a resource DELETE Remove a resource

docs.title: Aligned columns
docs.text:
| Left | Center | Right |
|:-----|:------:|------:|
| L    | C      | R     |
| left | center | right |
Left Center Right L C R left center right

docs.title: Wide tables
docs.text:
For tables with many columns, wrap them in a scrollable container:

<div class="md-typeset__scrollwrap"><div class="md-typeset__table">

| Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 | Column 4 | Column 5 |
|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Data     | Data     | Data     | Data     | Data     |

</div></div>

docs.title: Tables with code
docs.text:
| Extension | Syntax | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `admonition` | `!!! note` | Callout boxes |
| `attr_list` | `{: .class }` | HTML attributes |
| `pymdownx.highlight` | ` ```python ` | Code highlighting |
Extension Syntax Description admonition !!! note Callout boxes attr_list {: .class } HTML attributes pymdownx.highlight ```python Code highlighting

| Feature | Guide | Reference |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| Colors | [Setup](../setup/changing-the-colors.md) | — |
| Fonts | [Setup](../setup/changing-the-fonts.md) | — |
| Admonitions | — | [Reference](admonitions.md) |
| Code blocks | — | [Reference](code-blocks.md) |
Feature Guide Reference Colors Setup — Fonts Setup — Admonitions — Reference Code blocks — Reference

docs.title: CSV tables
docs.text:
For complex data, consider using the tables extension or importing from CSV:

markdown_extensions:
  - tables

docs.title: Styling
docs.text:
Apply CSS classes to tables:

| Header 1 | Header 2 |
|----------|----------|
| Cell 1   | Cell 2   |
{: .highlight }

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Admonitions
  • Formatting

docs.title: Diagrams
docs.text: DocsForge integrates Mermaid.js for creating diagrams with text.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Mermaid support is enabled by default:

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.superfences:
      custom_fences:
        - name: mermaid
          class: mermaid
          format: !!python/name:pymdownx.superfences.fence_code_format

docs.title: Flowchart
docs.text:
``` mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start] --> B{Is it?}
    B -->|Yes| C[OK]
    C --> D[Rethink]
    D --> B
    B ---->|No| E[End]

``` mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start] --> B{Is it?}
    B -->|Yes| C[OK]
    C --> D[Rethink]
    D --> B
    B ---->|No| E[End]

docs.title: Sequence diagram
docs.text:
``` mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant DocsForge
    participant GitHub

    User->>DocsForge: Write documentation
    User->>DocsForge: Run build
    DocsForge-->>User: Static site
    User->>GitHub: git push
    GitHub-->>User: Deployed site

``` mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant DocsForge
    participant GitHub

    User->>DocsForge: Write documentation
    User->>DocsForge: Run build
    DocsForge-->>User: Static site
    User->>GitHub: git push
    GitHub-->>User: Deployed site

docs.title: Class diagram
docs.text:
``` mermaid
classDiagram
    class Site {
        +String name
        +String url
        +build()
        +serve()
    }
    class Theme {
        +String name
        +configure()
    }
    Site --> Theme : uses

``` mermaid
classDiagram
    class Site {
        +String name
        +String url
        +build()
        +serve()
    }
    class Theme {
        +String name
        +configure()
    }
    Site --> Theme : uses

docs.title: State diagram
docs.text:

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Draft
    Draft --> Review: Submit
    Review --> Published: Approve
    Review --> Draft: Reject
    Published --> [*]

``` mermaid

    Draft --> Review: Submit

    Review --> Draft: Reject
    Published --> [*]

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Data tables
  • Formatting

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text: DocsForge extends standard Markdown with additional inline formatting options.

docs.title: Highlight
docs.text:
Mark text with ==highlighted==:

This is ==highlighted text== for emphasis.

This is highlighted text for emphasis.

docs.title: Subscript
docs.text:
Use ~subscript~:

H~2~O is the chemical formula for water.

H2O is the chemical formula for water.

docs.title: Superscript
docs.text:
Use ^superscript^:

The area of a circle is πr^2^.

The area of a circle is πr2.

docs.title: Inserted and deleted text
docs.text:
Use ++inserted++ and ~~deleted~~:

This is ++new++ and this is ~~old~~.

This is ++new++ and this is old.

docs.title: Keyboard keys
docs.text:
Use ++key++ syntax:

Press ++ctrl+alt+delete++ to restart.

Press ++cmd+shift+3++ to take a screenshot.

Press Ctrl+Alt+Del to restart.

Press Cmd+Shift+3 to take a screenshot.

docs.title: Mark
docs.text:
Use ==marked== text (alias for highlight):

==Important==: Read this section carefully.

Important: Read this section carefully.

docs.title: Critic markup
docs.text:
Track changes in drafts:

This is deleted and this is inserted.

docs.title: Smart symbols
docs.text:
Type common symbols easily:

Input Output (c) © (tm) ™ (r) ® 1/2 ½ 1/4 ¼ +- ± --> → <-- ← ==> ⇒ <== ⇐ ... …

docs.title: HTML entities
docs.text:
Use any HTML entity directly:

© ™ ® — – …

© ™ ® — – …

docs.title: Custom CSS classes
docs.text:
Apply CSS classes to inline elements:

[Text with class]{: .custom-class }

Or use attr_list extension for more control.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Icons & Emojis
  • Tooltips

docs.title: Icons & Emojis
docs.text: DocsForge includes 8,000+ Material Design icons and 3,000+ Twemoji emojis.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
Use :material-icon-name: syntax:

:material-heart: I love DocsForge!

I love DocsForge!

docs.title: Finding icons
docs.text:
Browse the Material Design Icons library. Common categories:

  • :material-home: —
  • :material-settings: — :material-settings:
  • :material-code-braces: —
  • :material-book-open: —
  • :material-github: —
  • :material-rocket-launch: —
  • :material-check-circle: —
  • :material-alert-circle: —

docs.title: In admonitions
docs.text: Icons appear automatically in admonition titles.

docs.title: In buttons and links
docs.text:
[Get started :material-arrow-right:](getting-started.md)

Get started

docs.title: Sized icons
docs.text:
Apply CSS classes for sizing:

:material-heart:{ .lg } Large heart

docs.title: Emojis
docs.text:
Use standard emoji shortcodes:

:smile: :thumbsup: :rocket: :fire: :star: :warning:

docs.title: Font Awesome icons
docs.text:
Font Awesome brands are also available:

:fontawesome-brands-github: GitHub
:fontawesome-brands-python: Python
:fontawesome-brands-docker: Docker

GitHub Python Docker

docs.title: Custom icons
docs.text:
Add your own icons in docs/assets/icons/:

extra:
  icon:
    logo: assets/logo.png

docs.title: Icon in headers
docs.text: ## :material-rocket: Getting Started

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Icon support is enabled by default:

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.emoji:
      emoji_generator: !!python/name:material.extensions.emoji.to_svg
      emoji_index: !!python/name:material.extensions.emoji.twemoji

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Images
  • Lists

docs.title: Images
docs.text: DocsForge handles images with enhanced styling options.

docs.title: Basic image
docs.text:
Standard Markdown syntax:

![Alt text](assets/images/screenshot.png)

docs.title: Image with caption
docs.text:
Use figure markup for captions:

<figure markdown="span">
  ![Screenshot](assets/images/screenshot.png)
  <figcaption>Documentation site preview</figcaption>
</figure>

docs.title: Left aligned
docs.text: ![Image](assets/images/screenshot.png){ align=left }

docs.title: Right aligned
docs.text: ![Image](assets/images/screenshot.png){ align=right }

docs.title: Center aligned
docs.text: ![Image](assets/images/screenshot.png){ align=center }

docs.title: Image size
docs.text:
Set width and height:

![Image](assets/images/screenshot.png){ width="300" }

![Image](assets/images/screenshot.png){ width="50%" }

docs.title: Lazy loading
docs.text:
Images load lazily by default. To disable for above-the-fold images:

![Hero image](assets/images/hero.png){ loading=eager }

docs.title: Shadow
docs.text:
Add a shadow effect:

![Image](assets/images/screenshot.png){ .shadow }

docs.title: Linking images
docs.text:
Make an image a link:

[![Alt text](assets/images/screenshot.png)](https://example.com)

docs.title: Responsive images
docs.text: DocsForge automatically makes images responsive. They scale to fit their container and don't overflow.

docs.title: Image formats
docs.text:
Recommended formats:

Format Use case PNG Screenshots, diagrams with transparency JPEG Photos, complex images WebP All of the above (smaller file size) SVG Icons, logos, simple diagrams

docs.title: Optimization tips
docs.text:
1. Use WebP when possible (smaller than PNG/JPEG)
  2. Compress images before adding to docs
  3. Use SVG for logos and icons
  4. Keep images under 200KB when possible
  5. Use descriptive alt text for accessibility

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Icons & Emojis
  • Lists

docs.title: Lists
docs.text: DocsForge supports all standard list types plus extended syntax.

docs.title: Unordered lists
docs.text:
- Item 1
- Item 2
- Item 3
  • Item 1
  • Item 2
  • Item 3

docs.title: Ordered lists
docs.text:
1. First item
2. Second item
3. Third item
  1. First item
  2. Second item
  3. Third item

docs.title: Nested lists
docs.text:
- Parent item
  - Child item 1
  - Child item 2
    - Grandchild item
- Another parent
  • Parent item
  • Child item 1
  • Child item 2
    • Grandchild item
  • Another parent

docs.title: Task lists
docs.text:
Enable with pymdownx.tasklist:

- [x] Completed task
- [ ] Incomplete task
- [ ] Another task
  • Completed task
  • Incomplete task
  • Another task

docs.title: Definition lists
docs.text:
Enable with def_list:

Term 1
:   Definition of term 1

Term 2
:   Definition of term 2
:   Another definition for term 2
Term 1 Definition of term 1 Term 2 Definition of term 2 Another definition for term 2

docs.title: Fancy task lists
docs.text:
Custom checkbox styling is enabled by default:

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.tasklist:
      custom_checkbox: true

This renders checkboxes with Material Design styling.

docs.title: List with code blocks
docs.text:
Lists can contain code blocks:

1. First step
   ``` bash
   echo "Hello"
   ```
2. Second step
   ``` bash
   echo "World"
   ```
  1. First step 
    echo "Hello"
  2. Second step 
    echo "World"

docs.title: List with admonitions
docs.text:
- Item with a note
    !!! note
        Important detail about this item
- Another item
  • Item with a note !!! note Important detail about this item
  • Another item

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Formatting
  • Tooltips

docs.title: Tooltips
docs.text: Tooltips show additional information when hovering over text.

docs.title: Abbreviations
docs.text:
Define abbreviations that show full text on hover:

The HTML specification is maintained by the W3C.

*[HTML]: Hyper Text Markup Language
*[W3C]: World Wide Web Consortium

The HTML specification is maintained by the W3C.

docs.title: Footnotes
docs.text:
Add footnotes with references:

DocsForge is a self-contained documentation build system.[^1]

[^1]: All dependencies are vendored into the project repository.

DocsForge is a self-contained documentation build system.1

docs.title: Content tooltips
docs.text:
Enable with content.tooltips feature:

theme:
  features:
    - content.tooltips

This adds hover tooltips for abbreviations and links throughout the site.

docs.title: Link tooltips
docs.text:
Use title attribute for link tooltips:

[DocsForge](https://github.com/QQSHI13/docsforge "Self-contained documentation builds")

docs.title: Definition lists as tooltips
docs.text:
Definition terms can serve as glossary entries:

DocsForge
:   A self-contained documentation build system with vendored dependencies.
DocsForge A self-contained documentation build system with vendored dependencies.

docs.title: Magic links
docs.text:
Automatic link conversion for GitHub references:

See issue #123 and PR #456 for details.

Enable with:

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.magiclink:
      repo_url_shorthand: true
      user: QQSHI13
      repo: docsforge-docs

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Formatting
  • Icons & Emojis

  1. All dependencies are vendored into the project repository. ↩

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: DocsForge's setup guides help you customize every aspect of your documentation site. All configuration happens in docsforge.yml.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The following guides cover the most common customization scenarios:

  • Changing the colors

    Customize primary, accent, and background colors. Configure light and dark mode palettes.

  • Changing the fonts

    Use Google Fonts or custom font files for text and code.

  • Changing the language

    Set the site language, configure search stemming, and RTL support.

  • Setting up navigation

    Tabs, sections, indexes, footer links, and table of contents.

  • Setting up site search

    Configure search behavior, separators, and result presentation.

  • Setting up site analytics

    Add Plausible or Google Analytics to track page views.

  • Setting up social cards

    Configure Open Graph and Twitter Card meta tags for rich link previews.

  • Building an optimized site

    Enable HTML minification, asset compression, and build optimization.

  • Adding a git repository

    Link to your source repo with "Edit this page" buttons.

docs.title: Configuration file
docs.text:
All customization is done in docsforge.yml. DocsForge loads all plugins and Markdown extensions automatically — you only configure what you want to customize.

docsforge.yml
site_name: My Project
site_url: https://example.com/docs/
site_author: Your Name
site_description: Documentation for My Project

repo_name: username/repo
repo_url: https://github.com/username/repo

copyright: Copyright © 2025 Your Name

theme:
  name: material
  features:
    - navigation.tabs
    - navigation.sections
    - navigation.top
    - search.highlight
    - search.suggest
    - content.code.copy
  palette:
    - media: "(prefers-color-scheme: light)"
      scheme: default
      primary: teal
      accent: teal
      toggle:
        icon: material/brightness-7
        name: Switch to dark mode
    - media: "(prefers-color-scheme: dark)"
      scheme: slate
      primary: black
      accent: teal
      toggle:
        icon: material/brightness-4
        name: Switch to light mode
  font:
    text: Roboto
    code: Roboto Mono

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Pick a guide above to customize a specific aspect of your site. Each guide includes copy-paste-ready configuration examples.

docs.title: Adding a git repository
docs.text: Link your documentation to its source repository. This adds an "Edit this page" button and shows the repository name in the header.

docs.title: Basic configuration
docs.text:
repo_name: username/repo
repo_url: https://github.com/username/repo

docs.title: Edit button
docs.text:
Enable the "Edit this page" button:

theme:
  features:
    - content.action.edit

This adds a pencil icon that links directly to the source file on GitHub (or your provider).

docs.title: View source button
docs.text:
Enable the "View source" button alongside edit:

theme:
  features:
    - content.action.edit
    - content.action.view

docs.title: Custom provider
docs.text:
DocsForge supports any Git provider that follows the GitHub URL pattern:

repo_url: https://gitlab.com/username/repo
# or
repo_url: https://bitbucket.org/username/repo
# or
repo_url: https://gitea.example.com/username/repo

docs.title: Repository icon
docs.text:
The repository icon is shown in the header. It's automatically determined from the URL:

  • github.com → GitHub icon
  • gitlab.com → GitLab icon
  • bitbucket.org → Bitbucket icon

docs.title: Custom edit path
docs.text:
If your docs live in a subdirectory of the repository:

edit_uri: edit/main/docs/

The full edit URL becomes: https://github.com/username/repo/edit/main/docs/page.md

docs.title: Complete example
docs.text:
repo_name: myproject/docs
repo_url: https://github.com/myproject/docs
edit_uri: edit/main/docs/

theme:
  features:
    - content.action.edit
    - content.action.view

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Publishing your site
  • Setup overview

docs.title: Building an optimized site
docs.text: DocsForge includes the minify plugin by default. It compresses HTML, CSS, and JavaScript at build time.

docs.title: What's optimized automatically
docs.text:
The minify plugin strips: - Whitespace and newlines from HTML - HTML comments - Optional attribute quotes - CSS and JavaScript whitespace

This runs on every build with no configuration needed.

docs.title: Image optimization
docs.text:
For best results, optimize images before adding them to your docs:

# Using pngquant for PNGs
pngquant --quality=70-90 docs/assets/images/*.png

# Using cwebp for WebP conversion
cwebp -q 80 image.png -o image.webp

docs.title: Privacy plugin (external assets)
docs.text:
The privacy plugin downloads and caches external assets (like Google Fonts) during the build. This means: - No CDN calls at runtime - Faster page loads - Works offline - Better privacy for your users

Runs automatically on every build.

docs.title: Build output
docs.text:
docsforge build

The site/ directory contains the optimized static site, ready for deployment.

docs.title: Changing the colors
docs.text: DocsForge uses the Material theme's color system. Customize colors in docsforge.yml under the theme.palette key.

docs.title: Color palette
docs.text: A color palette defines the colors used throughout your site. You can define multiple palettes and let users switch between them (e.g., light and dark mode).

docs.title: Minimal configuration
docs.text:
theme:
  palette:
    primary: teal
    accent: teal

docs.title: Primary colors
docs.text:
The primary color is used for the header, navigation, links, and interactive elements.

Color name Value red #F44336 pink #E91E63 purple #9C27B0 deep purple #673AB7 indigo #3F51B5 blue #2196F3 light blue #03A9F4 cyan #00BCD4 teal #009688 green #4CAF50 light green #8BC34A lime #CDDC39 yellow #FFEB3B amber #FFC107 orange #FF9800 deep orange #FF5722 brown #795548 grey #9E9E9E blue grey #607D8B black #000000 white #FFFFFF

docs.title: Accent colors
docs.text:
The accent color is used for hover states, active elements, and emphasis.

Same values as primary colors, except grey, brown, blue grey, black, and white.

docs.title: Light and dark mode
docs.text:
Configure multiple palettes for automatic and manual switching:

theme:
  palette:
    # Auto-detect system preference
    - media: "(prefers-color-scheme: light)"
      scheme: default
      primary: teal
      accent: teal
      toggle:
        icon: material/brightness-7
        name: Switch to dark mode
    - media: "(prefers-color-scheme: dark)"
      scheme: slate
      primary: black
      accent: teal
      toggle:
        icon: material/brightness-4
        name: Switch to light mode

docs.title: Schemes
docs.text:
• default — Light mode with white background
  • slate — Dark mode with dark grey background

docs.title: Custom colors
docs.text:
Use any hex color value:

theme:
  palette:
    primary: "#1E90FF"
    accent: "#FF6B6B"

docs.title: Code block colors
docs.text:
Code block themes are tied to the palette scheme:

  • default scheme → Light code highlighting (light background)
  • slate scheme → Dark code highlighting (dark background)

This happens automatically. No configuration needed.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Changing the fonts
  • Setting up navigation

docs.title: Changing the fonts
docs.text: DocsForge supports Google Fonts out of the box. Configure fonts in docsforge.yml under the theme.font key.

docs.title: Google Fonts
docs.text:
theme:
  font:
    text: Roboto
    code: Roboto Mono

docs.title: Supported font weights
docs.text:
DocsForge loads regular (400) and bold (700) weights automatically. For font families that need different weight names:

theme:
  font:
    text: "Open Sans"  # Quotes needed for multi-word names
    code: "Fira Code"

docs.title: Disabling font loading
docs.text:
If you want to use system fonts or load fonts yourself:

theme:
  font: false

docs.title: Recommended font pairings
docs.text:
• Roboto + Roboto Mono

    The default pairing. Clean, modern, excellent for technical docs.

  • Inter + JetBrains Mono

    Inter is optimized for screen readability. JetBrains Mono has ligatures and clear distinction between similar characters.

  • Open Sans + Source Code Pro

    Friendly and approachable. Great for community-oriented projects.

  • Lato + Fira Code

    Warm and professional. Fira Code adds programming ligatures.

docs.title: Custom fonts
docs.text:
To use fonts not available on Google Fonts, load them via custom CSS:

extra_css:
  - assets/stylesheets/custom.css
docs/assets/stylesheets/custom.css
@font-face {
  font-family: "My Font";
  src: url("../fonts/my-font.woff2") format("woff2");
  font-weight: 400;
}

:root {
  --md-text-font: "My Font";
}

Place your font files in docs/assets/fonts/.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Changing the colors
  • Changing the language

docs.title: Changing the language
docs.text: Set the site language in docsforge.yml. This affects search stemming, typography (e.g., Chinese justification), and RTL layout.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
extra:
  alternate:
    - name: English
      link: /
      lang: en
    - name: 中文
      link /zh/
      lang: zh

Or for a single language site, the language is primarily configured via the theme:

theme:
  language: en

docs.title: Supported languages
docs.text:
DocsForge (via the Material theme) supports 60+ languages:

  • en — English
  • zh — Chinese (Simplified)
  • zh-Hant — Chinese (Traditional)
  • ja — Japanese
  • ko — Korean
  • de — German
  • fr — French
  • es — Spanish
  • pt — Portuguese
  • pt-BR — Portuguese (Brazil)
  • it — Italian
  • ru — Russian
  • ar — Arabic
  • hi — Hindi
  • And 50+ more

docs.title: Search language
docs.text:
Search stemming is language-aware. The search plugin automatically uses the configured language:

plugins:
  - search:
      lang:
        - en
        - de

Multiple languages can be specified for multilingual search.

docs.title: Right-to-left (RTL) support
docs.text:
For Arabic, Hebrew, and other RTL languages, DocsForge automatically adjusts layout direction when the language is set:

theme:
  language: ar

docs.title: Custom translations
docs.text:
You can override individual translation strings:

extra:
  social:
    - icon: fontawesome/brands/github
      link: https://github.com/username/repo

Or create a custom translation file in docs/locales/xx/LC_MESSAGES/messages.po.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Setting up navigation
  • Setting up site search

docs.title: Setting up navigation
docs.text: DocsForge provides rich navigation options. Configure them in docsforge.yml under theme.features.

docs.title: Navigation structure
docs.text:
By default, DocsForge builds navigation from your docs/ directory structure:

docs/
├── index.md
├── getting-started.md
├── setup/
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── colors.md
│   └── fonts.md
└── reference/
    ├── index.md
    └── markdown.md

This creates: - Home - Getting started - Setup (expandable section) - Setup overview (index page) - Colors - Fonts - Reference (expandable section) - Reference overview (index page) - Markdown

docs.title: Top-level navigation tabs
docs.text:
Convert top-level sections into tabs:

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.tabs

Tip

Combine with navigation.tabs.sticky to keep tabs visible when scrolling: 

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.tabs
    - navigation.tabs.sticky

docs.title: Section indexes
docs.text:
Make section headers clickable, linking to their index.md:

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.indexes

docs.title: Expand sections by default
docs.text:
theme:
  features:
    - navigation.expand

docs.title: Section grouping
docs.text:
Group navigation items into collapsible sections (enabled by default):

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.sections

docs.title: Back-to-top button
docs.text:
Show a "back to top" button when scrolling down:

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.top

docs.title: Footer navigation
docs.text:
Add previous/next page links at the bottom of each page:

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.footer

docs.title: Follow active anchor
docs.text:
Highlight the current section in the table of contents as you scroll:

theme:
  features:
    - toc.follow

docs.title: Integrate with navigation
docs.text:
Move the table of contents into the navigation sidebar (for narrow layouts):

theme:
  features:
    - toc.integrate

docs.title: Custom navigation
docs.text:
Define navigation explicitly in docsforge.yml:

nav:
  - Home: index.md
  - Getting Started:
    - Installation: getting-started.md
    - Quick start: quick-start.md
  - Setup:
    - setup/index.md
    - Colors: setup/colors.md
    - Fonts: setup/fonts.md

docs.title: Navigation tracking
docs.text:
Highlight the current page in the navigation:

theme:
  features:
    - navigation.tracking

Enabled by default.

docs.title: Complete navigation example
docs.text:
theme:
  features:
    - navigation.tabs
    - navigation.sections
    - navigation.indexes
    - navigation.top
    - navigation.footer
    - navigation.tracking
    - toc.follow

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Setting up site search
  • Setting up site analytics

docs.title: Setting up site analytics
docs.text: Add analytics to understand how readers use your documentation.

docs.title: Plausible Analytics
docs.text:
Plausible is privacy-friendly, lightweight, and open source.

extra:
  analytics:
    provider: plausible
    property: docs.example.com

docs.title: Google Analytics
docs.text:
extra:
  analytics:
    provider: google
    property: G-XXXXXXXXXX  # Your Measurement ID

docs.title: Custom analytics
docs.text:
For other analytics providers, add the tracking script via extra JavaScript:

extra_javascript:
  - assets/javascripts/analytics.js
docs/assets/javascripts/analytics.js
// Your custom analytics initialization

docs.title: Privacy considerations
docs.text:
DocsForge is designed with privacy in mind:

  • Search is client-side (no search data leaves the browser)
  • No external fonts are loaded by default (all vendored)
  • No external JavaScript except what you explicitly add
  • Works fully offline with no network requests

If you add analytics, consider: - Using privacy-focused providers (Plausible, Fathom, GoatCounter) - Adding a privacy policy if required by your jurisdiction - Respecting Do Not Track signals

docs.title: Disabling analytics in development
docs.text: The DocsForge development server (docsforge serve) does not include analytics scripts. They are only added during production builds.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Setting up social cards
  • Building an optimized site

docs.title: Setting up site search
docs.text: DocsForge includes a powerful client-side search engine. It's enabled by default via the search plugin.

docs.title: Basic setup
docs.text:
Search is enabled by default:

plugins:
  - search

docs.title: Search separator
docs.text:
Control how search terms are split into tokens:

plugins:
  - search:
      separator: '[\s\u200b\-_,:!=\[\]()"`\/]+|\.(?!\d)|&[lg]t;|(?!\b)(?=[A-Z][a-z])'

The default separator splits on: - Whitespace and zero-width spaces - Hyphens, underscores, commas, colons - CamelCase boundaries (e.g., MyClass → My + Class)

docs.title: Language
docs.text:
Configure search stemming for your language:

plugins:
  - search:
      lang: en

Supported languages include: en, de, es, fr, ja, pt, ru, zh.

docs.title: Search features
docs.text: Enable in theme.features:

docs.title: Highlighting
docs.text:
Highlight matching terms in search results:

theme:
  features:
    - search.highlight

docs.title: Suggestions
docs.text:
Show autocomplete suggestions as you type:

theme:
  features:
    - search.suggest

docs.title: Share search
docs.text:
Allow users to share direct links to search results:

theme:
  features:
    - search.share

docs.title: Complete search configuration
docs.text:
theme:
  features:
    - search.highlight
    - search.suggest
    - search.share

plugins:
  - search:
      separator: '[\s\u200b\-_,:!=\[\]()"`\/]+|\.(?!\d)|&[lg]t;|(?!\b)(?=[A-Z][a-z])'
      lang: en

docs.title: Search behavior
docs.text:
• Instant: Results appear as you type, with no server round-trip
  • Fuzzy matching: Minor typos are tolerated
  • Stemming: Searching for "run" finds "running", "runs", etc.
  • Ranking: Results ordered by relevance (title matches rank higher)
  • Excerpts: Each result shows a snippet with context

docs.title: Excluding content from search
docs.text:
Add search.exclude front matter to hide a page:

---
search:
  exclude: true
---

Or exclude specific sections with HTML comments:

<!--search exclude-->
This content will not be indexed.
<!--end search exclude-->

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Setting up site analytics
  • Setting up social cards

docs.title: Setting up social cards
docs.text:
DocsForge includes basic OpenGraph metadata automatically. Every page generates:

  • og:title — Page title
  • og:description — Page description or site description
  • og:type — website
  • og:url — Canonical page URL
  • og:site_name — Site name

docs.title: Basic configuration
docs.text:
Set site-wide defaults in docsforge.yml:

site_name: My Project Docs
site_description: Complete documentation for My Project
site_url: https://docs.example.com/

docs.title: Customizing per page
docs.text:
Override metadata for individual pages using front matter:

---
description: Custom description for this page
---

docs.title: Verifying your cards
docs.text:
Test with these tools:

  • Facebook Sharing Debugger
  • LinkedIn Post Inspector

docs.title: Cards not appearing
docs.text:
1. Ensure site_url is set correctly in docsforge.yml
  2. Your site must be publicly accessible (validators can't reach localhost)
  3. Platforms cache metadata — use the debugger tools to force a refresh

docs.title: Wrong image or description
docs.text:
1. Check page front matter for description overrides
  2. Ensure site_description is set in docsforge.yml
  3. First paragraph of each page is used as fallback description
